title: Outline Expansion Pattern
summary: The Outline Expansion Pattern addresses LLM size limitations by building content in pieces using a hierarchical outline. You generate a top-level outline, then selectively expand individual bullet points into sub-outlines or full content. The outline's numbered structure (3.1, 3.2, 3.5.2) makes it obvious how pieces fit together. This pattern combines Persona + Ask for Input + Tail Generation. Key challenge: maintaining context across pieces — you may need to paste earlier outline sections into the prompt to ensure cohesion when generating later sections.

key_concepts.definition: A prompt pattern for building large bodies of work by generating a hierarchical outline and then selectively expanding individual sections, working around LLM input/output size limitations.
key_concepts.why_it_matters: LLMs have context window limits. This pattern lets you build books, software, or any large project piece by piece while maintaining overall structure.

key_concepts.definition: The challenge of maintaining relevant context when working with LLMs that have limited input/output sizes, requiring strategic selection of what information to include in each prompt.
key_concepts.why_it_matters: As conversations grow long, the LLM 'forgets' earlier content. You must actively manage what context is available for each generation step.

key_concepts.definition: Using numbered outline structures (3.1, 3.2.1) where the numbering makes it obvious how pieces fit together without additional instructions.
key_concepts.why_it_matters: When generating pieces separately, you need to know how to reassemble them. Numbered outlines make assembly trivial.

patterns.name: Outline Expansion Pattern
patterns.intent: Build large bodies of work by generating and expanding hierarchical outlines, working around LLM size limitations.
patterns.template: Act as an outline expander. Generate a bullet point outline based on the input I give you and then ask me for the bullet point I should expand on. When I select a bullet point, create a new detailed outline for that section. At the end, ask me for what bullet point to expand next. Ask me for what to outline.
patterns.example_prompt: Act as an outline expander. Generate a bullet point outline based on the input I give you and then ask me for the bullet point you should expand on. Ask me for what to outline.
patterns.when_to_use: When building large projects (books, courses, software, reports) that exceed LLM context limits. The outline serves as a map for generating and assembling pieces.

techniques: Start with a top-level outline, then expand sections to the level of detail you need.
Use numbered sections (3.1, 3.2.1) so pieces are self-describing for assembly.
Copy-paste relevant outline sections into prompts to maintain context.
If the LLM forgets earlier content, paste the relevant earlier outline back into the prompt.
Think about forward and backward references — does section 3.2 need to know what's in section 1?
Combine with Menu Actions Pattern to create expand/write actions for your outline.

quiz_questions.question: What fundamental limitation does the Outline Expansion Pattern address?
options: A. LLMs can't generate creative content
B. LLMs have limited input/output sizes (context windows), making it impossible to generate or process very large documents in one shot
D. LLMs are too slow for large documents
quiz_questions.explanation: LLMs have context window limits on both input and output. The Outline Expansion Pattern works around this by generating content in pieces that fit within those limits.

quiz_questions.question: Why are numbered outline sections (3.1, 3.2.1) described as 'self-describing' for assembly?
options: A. Because the LLM can read the numbers
B. Because the numbering makes it obvious where each piece fits in the overall structure without additional instructions
C. Because numbers are easier to type
D. Because the LLM requires numbered sections
quiz_questions.explanation: When you generate section 3.2 separately, you know it goes under section 3 — the numbering itself tells you the hierarchy. No separate assembly instructions needed.

quiz_questions.question: What context management challenge arises when expanding section 3.2 if section 1 has been 'forgotten'?
options: A. The LLM will refuse to continue
B. You may need to paste section 1's outline back into the prompt so the LLM can ensure section 3.2 is consistent with earlier content
C. The LLM will automatically retrieve section 1
D. There is no challenge — the LLM remembers everything
quiz_questions.explanation: As conversations grow long, earlier content falls out of the context window. You must actively paste relevant earlier sections back into the prompt to maintain consistency.

raw_examples: Act as an outline expander. Generate a bullet point outline based on the input I give you and then ask me for the bullet point you should expand on. Ask me for what to outline.
